---
title: "Conclusions: What I Learned Reproducing a Nature Paper"
description: "A reflection on reproducing DeAbe+ — what worked, what didn't, and what comes next"
author: "Camila Ramirez"
date: "2026-04-01"
categories: [conclusions, reflection, deep learning, reproducibility]
draft: false
---

## What I Set Out To Do

For my senior project I attempted to reproduce the results of Guo et al. 
(2024) — a Nature Communications paper proposing DeAbe+, a deep learning 
pipeline for correcting optical aberrations in fluorescence microscopy 
images of *C. elegans* worm embryos.

My goals were:
1. Run the original code on the provided test data
2. Understand the mathematical theory behind the model
3. Reproduce the key results from the paper
4. Document everything publicly

## What I Actually Accomplished

The project took an unexpected but valuable turn:

**What worked:**
- ✅ Obtained real microscopy data directly from the authors
- ✅ Loaded and visualized *C. elegans* embryo images in Python
- ✅ Understood the full 3D-RCAN architecture mathematically
- ✅ Rebuilt the entire network from scratch in modern PyTorch (~100 lines)
- ✅ Trained on 5,600 real paired image crops overnight on Apple M3
- ✅ Produced visibly improved aberration correction results
- ✅ Documented everything in a public blog

**What didn't work:**
- ❌ Running the original TensorFlow 1.13 code on any modern platform
- ❌ Loading pretrained Keras weights into PyTorch
- ❌ Matching the paper's quantitative metrics (200 epochs vs 5)

## The Reproducibility Problem

The biggest unexpected finding was how difficult it was to run 
5-year-old deep learning code. The original code required:

- Python 3.7 (current: 3.13)
- TensorFlow 1.13 (unavailable since ~2021)
- NVIDIA GPU with CUDA 10.0 (I have Apple M3)

I patched 5 separate compatibility errors manually, contacted the 
original authors, and tried Google Colab — all before deciding to 
rewrite from scratch.

This experience highlights **software rot** — a growing crisis in 
computational science where published code becomes unrunnable within 
a few years due to rapidly evolving dependencies.

## The Math I Learned

Before this project I had never implemented a neural network from scratch.
Now I understand exactly how each operation connects to linear algebra:

- **Convolutions** = sliding 3D dot products (matrix multiplication)
- **Residual connections** = matrix addition with skip connections
- **Channel attention** = learned scaling using sigmoid and matrix multiplication
- **Gradient descent** = chain rule applied to millions of parameters

My linear algebra and multivariable calculus classes were directly 
applicable to understanding and building this network.

## Key Results

After training for 5 epochs on Apple M3:

![Aberration Correction Result](../05-pytorch-rewrite/trained_result_good.png)

The trained model produces visibly sharper cell membrane boundaries 
compared to the aberrated input. Individual cells are more clearly 
defined, and the membrane network structure is more distinct.

![Training Loss](../05-pytorch-rewrite/training_loss.png)

Loss decreased consistently from 11.3 → 10.5 over 5 epochs, 
confirming the model was learning.

## What I Would Do Differently

1. **Start with PyTorch from the beginning** — don't spend weeks 
   fighting old TensorFlow code
2. **Train for more epochs** — 5 epochs vs 200 means my results 
   are preliminary
3. **Use a larger model** — 154K parameters vs 1.5M in the original
4. **Contact authors earlier** — they were responsive and helpful

## Future Work

- Train for 50-200 epochs to approach paper's quality
- Convert original Keras weights to PyTorch format
- Implement Steps 2 and 3
